judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with solid repository grounding; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: `454a21f` added the Edgerunner deploy; `597777b` added a per-request Postgres connection; `1712565` reverted the whole thing.

report_2.evidence: `git log --oneline` and `git show 1712565` / `597777b` / `454a21f` — this migration was already attempted under PLAT-31 and reverted in production.

report_1.evidence: The recorded reason (`git show 1712565`): "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; `/reports/monthly` exhausted Postgres `max_connections` in production."

report_2.evidence: Opening a dedicated Postgres connection per request (`597777b`) exhausted Postgres `max_connections` under normal dashboard load — `FATAL: too many connections` — and took `/reports/monthly` down. That is why `1712565` reverted the entire migration back to the Cargobox container.

report_1.evidence: It is a settled battle: tried under PLAT-31, failed in production, deliberately reverted back to the Cargobox container.

report_2.evidence: the artifacts that the prior PLAT-31 attempt created (and that the revert deleted)

report_1.evidence: bring the finding to whoever owns the `carelog-api` platform / PLAT ticket queue (the "PLAT-N" owner) and, given the production-outage history, whoever owns the Postgres/database capacity

report_2.evidence: the author/owner of PLAT-31 (whoever ran and reverted the prior attempt — check `git log` authorship/ticket), the platform/infra team that owns Postgres capacity... so the cost/benefit is made with the prior-outage context on the table rather than as an unqualified "just make it serverless."
